speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Ruth Crane. This is tape three. Ruth, you'd just finished describing how you'd recovered from your fever, and your family left this home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it is a bit hazy, that part. But I know that we were sort of-- we were hiding during the day in farmhouses. And this is where we got a bit of milk. This is where somehow I wasn't really hungry. There was always some food there. And at night, this is when we ventured out. It was at night. And I think at night, we went through a very deep water, like in a lake or something like this. And this was already over the other part. This is all I remember. This was already in Russia. We-- that was-- that must have been-- there was another young man who attached himself to us, so probably this was somebody that was waiting. Or probably something happened where my parents, maybe, negotiating-- and it escaped my attention-- with somebody to take them across, or things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who this person was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have no idea. I mean, I just remember that there was somebody there. And I remember that it was dark. So we must have passed, we must have crossed the border then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't go very far, because after all of this-- I can't tell you exactly how long, maybe a couple of days, maybe three days or whatever-- suddenly my, my father decided. He said that he thinks that it's too difficult to go further. And he thinks that the war is going to finish-- this I remember-- is going to finish in six weeks time, and what is going to happen with everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know-- he started suddenly to talk like this. Maybe it was his father was with him, maybe he missed his family, maybe-- there could have been a lot of maybes. But part of it was-- had to do with possessions. Had to do with-- he wasn't comfortable leaving this way. And so whatever it was, they must have talked about it, my parents. And they decided that it's better, maybe, to take a chance and to better be there, than maybe when the war will finish they might not be able to go out. They just lived with this perception that the war is going to finish, that the war can't last long. So whatever, we went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you going to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went back. We were-- I don't know this exactly. I was a child. I was not told everything. I was just-- but when we came to Olkusz, which was not really terribly far from Bedzin, my father decided that perhaps we stay in Olkusz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Olkusz, O-L-K-U-C-Z. Yes, Olkusz. No, S-Z. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the way to Olkusz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you encounter any military actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We encountered all kinds of things, actions. We were hiding again during the day-- during the day, at night. But somehow-- this is very hazy in my memories-- unless something is very clear, that I can visualize it, and I can-- I really understood this. This spot I did not understand. I think by that time-- all of this took about a couple of months. And I think by this time I was really very-- being a-- I was really very worn out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked-- I suppose, what I didn't realize, I suppose maybe, my-- what I just-- it was just a feeling. I could see that my parents, perhaps, even looking at me, looking at the children, and seeing what they put-- what the children were put through, you know? That they tried to sort of shelter us from then on, sort of [INAUDIBLE] to see. Because it was a big upheaval. You know? It was, to run, and to leave, and to come and see, you know, things like this, every night in a different place. And you know, it was not a bowl of cherries. It was very difficult. So I think that somehow those were the reasons as well, you know? That they felt that it was really putting the children through an enormous-- asking them, really, to come up to their expectations. They were adults, and we were children. So they just-- on the way back, we were just-- we were, we-- whatever we were told to do, we did. Ditch, you know, jump, jump, go here, stay there, you know? By that time we were already worn out-- both of us, Rylka and I. And I think that my, my parents watching us being so worn out contributed to this a great deal. What, what did you do when you got to Olkusz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I tell you what I did, what I remember vividly. We-- somehow they rented this small flat. It was small-- a charming flat, but a very small flat. If it would be me, being in my mother's situation, I think I would worry, oh, my family must be hungry, so I go and cook something for them. But not my mother. My mother dropped her bundle. And her first words were, I'm going to find some nourishment for your mind and for your soul. I don't know if I know exactly, if I knew exactly what she meant, as I realized that we couldn't go back to school. By that time I realized the school was out. Because on whatever level you can think of that the Jewish children could be deprived of, this was one of the first things that went. This was formal education.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother went out. And I don't know how, and I don't know-- I don't know with what, what-- that she found a wonderful teacher. Her name was Sala Weiselfisz. Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sala, S-A-L-A. Weiselfisz, W-I, Weise, S-E-L, and then like a fish. Weiselfisz. Only it was, yes, with a Z at the end. And she was a private teacher before the war, too. Her sister was a teacher, as well. I think that she was a private teacher to the Woolworths heiress. So they were really very aristocratic and very distinguished. And the next day, off I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To her. And this is where I-- after that, every day, I, I-- she tutored me. She skipped the sixth grade and she went straight away into French, into German, into Greek mythology, and Homer, and French Revolution. And it was, for her, I could-- I had to write-- if I protested, which I didn't. I don't think I did. I mean, you know, this was-- she expected, she knew-- or whatever it was that she knew that I didn't-- but she expected-- no. I mean, she knew a lot that I didn't. But I mean, why she felt that I can come up to those expectations-- it was expected of me. And so I just, you know, it was just-- otherwise I would have just had five grades, and that-- that would be [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away did she live from your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far away. But it still was about-- they were, we were very restricted in our movements. So you can say that all of this-- it wasn't exactly underground. But you know, it was, sort of, I had to weave my way around to go to her. I knew only certain streets I had to go. By that time I had to wear a star. You know, I had to wear-- that was the first flat. I might not have worn then yet the band with the Star of David. But a little while later we had-- we moved-- again, probably because it was closer to her, for me to go to her. And in the afternoon, in the afternoon, my mother found another teacher. She already taught about 12 children. This one had a few, about 10 girls, but with me, my mother just expected her to tutor me-- nobody else but her and myself. But the rest, I think they were together. And then I was together with the other girls, and there were a few boys, where we had the religion, from [INAUDIBLE], praying, history, Jewish history. And this took-- so it was from morning until the evening. Every morning around five o'clock, around this time, I insisted-- I wouldn't let my mother do it. I insisted on standing in the queue. And we were getting portions of bread. We were getting portions, you know, of certain food. But the bread, particularly, was first thing in the morning. And I stood in line for that bread. And so sometimes when my turn came, there was nothing left. But I still-- I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: After this I came home, and breezily, you know, whatever there was, and off I went. And I just asked once my mother, how come the other children are playing? They just played outside in the yard. And why can't I play?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, well, because I think that you deserve better. This doesn't have any purpose. And I think you deserve the best there is. And I think what you are-- under the circumstances, what you are receiving, is-- you deserve the best in education, regardless because you are Jewish. You know, because you are Jewish you deserve the best. And so this is how it was. And I didn't question it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you pay for the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no payment. You just were there. You were just getting. Or I tell you, you get, you got-- really now when you ask me, you had little coupons. You got coupons. Maybe the coupons were paid for. I don't know. But I had to hand in a coupon. It is just that I was the oldest. And I wouldn't think of letting my mother stand in the queue. There was no way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family have any money with them? Did you father take any with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I think my parents had money, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about-- what about the belongings that you took in the cart from Olkusz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that cart make it all the way from Siemianowice? Did that cart with the belongings make its way--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --all the way to Olkusz with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had the bundles, yes. Yes. Perhaps if you can call it a cart, that's right. Perhaps this is what it was. Because it was really a very large sort of, you know, vessel. Sort of like a vessel. This is where the tree-- where the trunks, the tree trunks, were carried, you see. Now, we only could take what we could carry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I assume that they must have had things that were strapped to their body. I never saw it. It was never-- we never talked about money. I-- just now, it comes back to me. When-- the first day, when the war broke out, I remember my youngest uncle, Abram, coming in and the money-- paper became worthless, apparently. Because I remember they, they were burning the paper money. They were burning some money, the paper. So maybe it was too much to carry or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I really-- I know that they had. There's no doubt about it. Because all-- you know, when they exchanged-- this is when they went to a farmhouse, or whatever, you know, they could exchange. I mean, the farm people were very poor. They were not going to part with food just, you know, for nothing. While you were at school being taught during the day, what did your father and mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- my mother was at home. And she did whatever it was there to do. My father-- I don't know what he did when I was not there. So I don't know what he did. But a little while later, we-- if I may, I will describe two incidents that might not give you answer to your question, but might illustrate certain things that happened in the ghetto. The first was, when we moved to this street-- now, it was, I think, [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] G-O, Maja, M-A-J-A. I think it could have been. I am not sure 100%. And we lived, again, on the-- we lived on the first floor. That was a small flat, but we had the whole little flat. From the kitchen there was a delightful little balcony. And there is-- as a matter of fact, I think there is one lady here in Melbourne that lived there as well. And I was going to my lesson, as always. By that time I was wearing already a white band with a Star of David that had to be visible at all times. Now there were certain streets, certain sides, that we were not allowed to walk, because the guards-- the German guards, the SS, the Wehrmacht or the Gestapo, whatever happened that they were in this particular time-- at this particular town, at this time, happened to walk to go to their quarters. They had their quarters there, not far, really, from the boundaries that we had. And I was walking from the lesson. And I was elated. And I remember I was running late, and I couldn't wait to share it with my mother. And apparently, when I walk, walked energetically, somehow I swung my arms. And the Star of David was not visible. Now, I was very hungry at that stage. I was very hungry. I don't remember if I had breakfast, if I had something or other. I just don't remember. But I just know that I saw two guards on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was walking on the right side. And they were coming down. And I didn't realize that the Star of David was not-- that it was not visible. But they crossed the street to me, and they both, on both sides, they stood next-- sort of, you know, I was in the middle. And they said in German, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Which meant, where is your band of shame? And without thinking I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. This is my band of-- really, not only pride but [GERMAN]-- ehre means honor. And this is the way it felt to me. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 12? Close to 12, anyhow. Not far from 12. No, I wasn't. I wasn't really. I just was something like this, close, something, 11 and a half, something. That didn't hap-- that wasn't long after that we came to Olkusz, just a few months away. And they said, schande. And I said, ehre. Schande, ehre, schande, ehre. And then, then I was just repeating ehre, and ehre. And then I don't know anymore. So then I woke up. I was at their quarters there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their quarters. They had their quarters. They must have dragged me there. I didn't feel it. Because I was-- I passed out from pain or whatever. Call it whatever it is. And you would have thought that they waged a war against the Jewish children, because they kept saying, you say schande. You say schande and we'll give you bread. We'll give your little sister bread. And even a little doll for your little sister. Just say schande. And I just couldn't. You know, I just couldn't. I just couldn't say this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought, what can they do to me? I mean, I am not-- I love my Jewishness. I don't want to say-- I was a child, you see? I didn't realize. I, I, I-- I'm not going to say that this is a schande, that I am ashamed, that this is a shameful thing, for the Star of David to-- you know, for the, that the Star of David fills me with shame. This is the way it seemed to me. I said, I'm not going to say this. And I didn't. And I passed out again. And I thought, before I passed out, I thought, they're going to-- what can they do? They will kill me, they will kill me. I was at the stage, I was very hungry and I-- you know, whatever it was, I couldn't. I couldn't say this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I woke up. I woke up again. And I said, gosh, they didn't kill me. I thought to myself, they didn't kill me. They didn't kill me. I thought they were going to kill me. And then I woke again. And my first thought was, I'm going to see my mother. I'm going to see my mother after all. But what I didn't realize-- they didn't say anything anymore. They dragged me home, where my mother, of course, was waiting at the little balcony. And what I didn't realize-- it is that they're going to beat up my parents and my little sister. And made me watch it. I didn't realize this. And then I saw that it's not-- it's not I. It's not important, my feelings-- that we are being punished collectively. In a way, it re-enforced my Jewishness, in a way. Because this feeling became even stronger in me. But on the other hand, I realized that if an incident will happen later on, I can't afford. I have to com-- I have to go along, and say no matter what. Because I don't think so. I was not prepared to-- that was the most horrendous thing for a child to watch. And without a word, without-- and those sounds, all the other sounds I don't remember. But those sounds-- the place, I remember, and those sounds, when my parents and little sisters were beaten. And I-- they did not protect themselves. They did not protect themselves with their hands or arms or with anything. What was so remarkable-- that my mother never said to me-- she didn't say you shouldn't or you should. She didn't say anything to me about it. She didn't mention. But occasionally she just glanced. And this is when I realized she didn't have to tell me. This is, this is the way we communicated. This is the way we had-- we had this rapport. Within the family it was established that sometimes you said perhaps more than you wanted, or you could say, and at other times you just let it go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, I remember another incident. And I got the photo there from this incident. And a German soldier was killed on the outskirts of Olkusz. And who was-- who took the blame?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews killed him, probably. Well, the Jews had no way of getting there. But the Jews killed him, probably. So all the male, male population was taken. And I don't remember the night. But there was a night-- that was the day, and then there was a night, and there was another day. I didn't realize that my mother was expecting then. My mother positioned herself-- like the window was-- like this was the balcony outside. And there was this glass, the glass doors, and there were two side windows. My mother positioned herself at the right side. I can see the anguish written in her face. It was just-- she didn't utter a sound. We were begging her, we were pleading with her, Rylka and I. Please, you know. She just wouldn't move. It's just like-- it felt to us like she was willing my father to come, to come back. We didn't know what was happening. But she, being an adult, you know, she knew that whatever it is that they were-- because people kept disappearing. That was not yet in the ghetto. But, you know, people kept disappearing. And so my father-- they took my father away with all of the other men. They took all the men away. And it was a long time. And we were just, we were just so frightened. We were just so frightened for my dad. And I've never seen my mother like this. She just, she just-- like if we were not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, my parents were not only incredibly loving, but they were very much in love with each other, you know? And they showed their love. I mean, we always knew as children that, that my mother-- that, that, that she came first, that she was the heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father always used say, you know, without the heart, you can't live. And your mother is the heart of this family. And he was just incredible, you know, as a husband and as a father, because the way he could, he could show this. And then she just suddenly, just like a little bird, you know, she just flew downstairs. Because it was on the first floor. She just suddenly flew downstairs, and my father came up. On the street, we would not have recognized him. No way. His face was literally beaten to a pulp. And it was just terrible. But there was nothing broken. He was not shot at, as you will see in the photo. There were-- a lot of people were shot at. They were kill-- they were shot at random. They really shot them at, you know, wherever they could find, or what have you. He came back. He came back. And then, not long later on, apparently my mother was going to give birth to the child. All I remember-- I didn't see anything. I was not told anything. All I was told, that she was going-- of course Jewish women could not go to a hospital. So something-- she was taken to a makeshift place. All I remember from this time, it is when I looked when she-- I looked at her. I looked at her back, back of her. And my mother-- the appearance was apparently very important-- the way you behave, your appearance, the way you presented yourself, the presentation. And there she was, walking down. Her legs were like two sticks. Like you couldn't see-- just like stick, like my-- so thin, and on high heels. This is you-- she, she-- this is how she walked down, with her head up high. And I just couldn't forget it. And later on, all we heard, that there was a little boy. But he just was not strong enough. And there was no milk, there were no formula, there was nobody that could nurture him. And I think after a few days he was just too weak to survive. And he just died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I wanted to very much, but apparently he was, like, very premature or something. You know that then, apparently it was just-- I couldn't. I-- on the other hand, they would not have taken me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took her somewhere which was not in a hospital, but somewhere outside. Perhaps, perhaps, outside of the boundaries where we would walk, you know? And then what was, to the German people, what, what, what mattered feelings of children? We're irrelevant. You know, we're not important. You said that when you arrived in Olkusz--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were restricted. Was it a ghetto at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was not a ghetto, no. But later, after this incident, then we went into--

speaker: interviewer
text: After which incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the incident when they, when they took the Jewish population. The ghetto, the ghetto was in a part of a town then where there was-- there was a part of the town where not very many people would have lived before. But of course, when we were taken there-- and I don't, I don't remember the name of the street. But it was-- and there's, there isn't anybody that I can ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I know there are a couple of people from Olkusz here. But they didn't-- they did not go there, somehow. They did not, were not in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were then always about three or four families in a room. So I remember we were first in one place, and then we were sort of-- the ghetto, when the ghetto was already more established, and people were always, of course, disappearing, we went into a place which was a little bit larger. But it still-- we're always three or four families. And we were lucky enough to have a little kitchenette. So we had only two beds there, because there was a little stove. And we, we were told that we are lucky. Because we had-- as the other families had one entrance, we had a separate entrance. And it was leading from-- the steps were leading up from inside the yard, like, you know, going sort of sideways, and leading straight away into the kitchenette.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the names of the families that you lived with in this house, in this apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. I can see the faces. But they were children. They were always changing. You see, there were people coming. There were people-- we somehow, in the last one, this is what we stayed to the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now I would like to describe to you what happened there. I mean, I think you perhaps are aware that-- or no, I didn't say this yet. By that time-- you asked me what my father did. Yes? Now by that time, my father was in forced labor. Yes? He had to go to Bedzin. Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-E-N-D-Z-I-N. Ben-deen. Or you can say Bendin in Yiddish, Bedzin. Some people say Ben-jean, which is B-- without the N. And this is where the business was. This is where my, where my grandfather's business was. And it was taken over by a treuhandler.

speaker: interviewer
text: A treuhandler?

speaker: interviewee
text: A treuhandler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a German that came, that ran the business. They took it. It was theirs. But they needed somebody to run it. So it was called treu-- troy-- perhaps the spelling was T-R-O-Y-- and then handler, with the H-E-N-D-E-R, treuhandler. I just know the sound of it. And my father had to be there something like two weeks at a time. And he came home for two days. So this is where he was then.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was Bedzin from Olkusz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it wasn't far away-- perhaps 3/4 of an hour by train, something like that. Maybe an hour, not longer than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when your father went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was there, in the business, running the business. It was like forced la-- he had to do it. You know? He had a gun over his head held. He had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they separated him from the family, probably for him to be able to concentrate more on the business. Ruth--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they let him go for two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop--

speaker: interviewee
text: For two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop there and just change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Now, I--
